scSidekick - Pathway Analysis: Single-Cell Scoring & Enrichment

10_pathway_enrichment.Rmd

Overview

This vignette covers three complementary approaches to pathway analysis at the single-cell level:

Function What it does
RunSCssGSEA() Score every cell against gene sets; test for group differences
PlotPathwayButterfly() Map cells onto a 2-D axis defined by exactly 4 pathways
RunEnrichment() Over-representation analysis (ORA) on a gene list or DEG table

These tools answer different questions:

  • RunSCssGSEA: “Which pathways are differentially active between my cell types, at the single-cell level?”
  • PlotPathwayButterfly: “Where does each cell sit in a 4-pathway state space? Do my cell types polarize toward specific states?”
  • RunEnrichment: “Given my list of significant DEGs, which pathways are over-represented?”

Part 1: RunSCssGSEA

1.1 Overview

RunSCssGSEA() computes a pathway activity score for every cell using either GSVA’s ssGSEA algorithm or UCell, then tests for significant differences between groups using ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis. Results are visualized as a mean z-score heatmap (pathways x groups) and optional per-pathway boxplots.

Gene-set input modes (pick one):

Mode Parameter Example
1. Custom gene list gene_sets list(MySet = c("GENE1","GENE2"))
2. DEG data frame deg_df RunWilcoxAUC or FindMarkers output
3. MSigDB collection gene_set_library "H" (Hallmark)
4. MSigDB search search_terms c("interferon","apoptosis")

Modes 3 and 4 can be combined.

1.2 Setup 

library(scSidekick)
library(Seurat)
library(SeuratData)

data("bmcite")
bmcite <- UpdateSeuratObject(bmcite)
bmcite <- PrepObject(bmcite,
  variables   = c("celltype.l1", "celltype.l2", "donor"),
  group.by    = "celltype.l1",
  output_dir  = tempdir(),
  object_name = "bmcite")

1.3 Mode 1: Custom gene sets

Pass a named list of gene vectors. Each element becomes one row in the heatmap.

RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by  = "celltype.l1",
  gene_sets = list(
    T_effector  = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG", "TNF"),
    T_exhausted = c("PDCD1", "HAVCR2", "LAG3", "TIGIT"),
    NK_cytotoxic = c("NCR1", "KLRB1", "GNLY", "NKG7")
  ),
  output_dir  = tempdir(),
  object_name = "bmcite")

1.4 Mode 2: DEG data frame

Build gene sets automatically from a DEG table. Each group’s top DEGs become one gene set. The deg_group_column can be a single column (groups are heatmap rows) or two columns (the first column is used to split rows on the heatmap).

# Get DEGs first
markers <- RunWilcoxAUC(bmcite, group.by = "celltype.l1")

RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by        = "celltype.l1",
  deg_df          = markers,
  deg_gene_column = "feature",
  deg_group_column = "group",
  deg_fc_column   = "logFC",
  deg_padj_column = "padj",
  deg_padj_cutoff = 0.05,
  deg_top_n       = 30L,
  output_dir      = tempdir(),
  object_name     = "bmcite")

1.5 Mode 3: MSigDB collection

Fetch an entire MSigDB collection and score all cells against it. Large collections (e.g. GO:BP with thousands of sets) should be filtered with search_terms or min.size to keep the run tractable.

RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by         = "celltype.l1",
  gene_set_library = "H",   # Hallmark
  min.size         = 10L,
  output_dir       = tempdir(),
  object_name      = "bmcite")

1.6 Mode 4: MSigDB keyword search

Search gene-set names with case-insensitive regex. Pass a character vector for OR logic, or a list of vectors for AND-groups.

# OR: any set mentioning "interferon" OR "apoptosis"
RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by      = "celltype.l1",
  search_terms  = c("interferon", "apoptosis"),
  pathway_sets  = list(Hallmark = list(category = "H")),
  output_dir    = tempdir(),
  object_name   = "bmcite")

# AND-group: sets mentioning BOTH "T_CELL" AND "ACTIVATION"
RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by     = "celltype.l1",
  search_terms = list(c("T_CELL", "ACTIVATION")),
  pathway_sets = list(
    C7 = list(category = "C7", subcategory = "IMMUNESIGDB")),
  output_dir   = tempdir(),
  object_name  = "bmcite")

1.7 Switching to UCell backend

The default scoring backend is GSVA’s ssGSEA. Use method = "ucell" for the UCell rank-based algorithm, which scales better to large datasets and avoids the GSVA memory overhead.

RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by         = "celltype.l1",
  gene_set_library = "H",
  method           = "ucell",
  ucell_max_rank   = 1500L,
  output_dir       = tempdir(),
  object_name      = "bmcite")

1.8 Downsampling large datasets

For objects with >50,000 cells, pass downsample to cap the number of cells per group before scoring.

RunSCssGSEA(large_obj,
  group.by         = "celltype",
  gene_set_library = "H",
  downsample       = 5000L,
  output_dir       = "./Figures/Pathways")

1.9 Customizing heatmap colors

The default is a 5-stop diverging palette centered at 0 (z = -2 to +2). Override with any circlize::colorRamp2 object.

# Classic blue-white-red
col_bwr <- circlize::colorRamp2(c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2),
                                 c("#2166ac", "#92c5de", "white",
                                   "#f4a582", "#d6604d"))

RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by         = "celltype.l1",
  gene_set_library = "H",
  heatmap_colors   = col_bwr,
  output_dir       = tempdir(),
  object_name      = "bmcite")

1.10 Resuming a run

resume = TRUE reloads a previously saved score CSV checkpoint, skipping re-scoring for the cells already processed.

RunSCssGSEA(bmcite,
  group.by         = "celltype.l1",
  gene_set_library = "H",
  resume           = TRUE,
  output_dir       = tempdir(),
  object_name      = "bmcite")

RunSCssGSEA parameter reference

Parameter Type Default Description
seurat_object Seurat required Annotated Seurat object
group.by character required Metadata column for grouping
split.by character or NULL NULL Run independently per level
gene_sets named list or NULL NULL Mode 1: custom gene lists
deg_df data frame or NULL NULL Mode 2: DEG table
gene_set_library character or NULL NULL Mode 3: MSigDB collection code
search_terms character/list or NULL NULL Mode 4: keyword filter
pathway_sets named list Hallmark MSigDB collections for Modes 3/4
method character "gsva" Scoring backend: "gsva" or "ucell"
downsample integer or NULL NULL Max cells per group
min.size integer 10 Min gene-set size after intersection
ssgsea.norm logical FALSE Normalize ssGSEA scores to range
ucell_max_rank integer 1500 UCell only: top genes considered
p_cutoff numeric 0.05 Adjusted p-value threshold for heatmap rows
show_only_significant logical TRUE Show only significant pathways
heatmap_colors colorRamp2 or NULL NULL Custom color function
heatmap_params named list list(...) Extra args to ComplexHeatmap::Heatmap()
add_boxplots logical TRUE Per-pathway boxplots
add_pvalues logical TRUE Add p-values to boxplots
resume logical FALSE Reload checkpoint CSV
caffeinate logical FALSE Prevent system sleep

Part 2: PlotPathwayButterfly

2.1 Overview

PlotPathwayButterfly() maps every cell onto a 2-D coordinate system defined by exactly four pathways arranged in quadrants. This makes cell state polarization visually immediate: cells that are high in the top-left pathway cluster in the upper-left of the plot; cells sitting at the center are in transition.

Quadrant layout:

              top-left | top-right
              ---------|----------
           bottom-left | bottom-right

The Y-axis separates the dominant axis (top vs bottom pathways); the X-axis gives the left-right balance within whichever axis dominates for each cell.

Output layout: one large overview panel (all cells colored by group.by) flanked by one small panel per group level (that group highlighted in color; remaining cells in gray).

This visualization is adapted from the scrabble R package (Suva Lab).

2.2 Basic call with MSigDB Hallmark pathways

Supply the four pathway names as they appear in the MSigDB collection. The pathway_sets argument specifies which collection to search.

PlotPathwayButterfly(
  seurat_object = bmcite,
  group.by      = "celltype.l1",
  quadrant_order = c(
    "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE",  # bottom-left
    "HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE",      # bottom-right
    "HALLMARK_G2M_CHECKPOINT",             # top-left
    "HALLMARK_MYC_TARGETS_V1"             # top-right
  ),
  pathway_sets  = list(Hallmark = list(category = "H")),
  output_dir    = tempdir()
)

2.3 Custom gene sets

When you have your own gene sets (not from MSigDB), pass them directly via gene_sets. The names must match quadrant_order.

PlotPathwayButterfly(
  seurat_object  = bmcite,
  group.by       = "celltype.l1",
  gene_sets      = list(
    Cytotoxic    = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG"),
    NaiveLike    = c("CCR7", "SELL", "TCF7"),
    Exhausted    = c("PDCD1", "LAG3", "HAVCR2"),
    Regulatory   = c("FOXP3", "IL2RA", "CTLA4")
  ),
  quadrant_order = c("NaiveLike", "Cytotoxic", "Regulatory", "Exhausted"),
  output_dir     = tempdir()
)

The names in quadrant_order map to the four quadrant positions: bottom-left, bottom-right, top-left, top-right.

2.4 Custom corner labels

By default, corner labels are auto-shortened from the pathway name (prefix and underscores removed). Override with quadrant_labels for cleaner display.

PlotPathwayButterfly(
  seurat_object  = bmcite,
  group.by       = "celltype.l1",
  gene_sets      = list(
    Cytotoxic    = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG"),
    NaiveLike    = c("CCR7", "SELL", "TCF7"),
    Exhausted    = c("PDCD1", "LAG3", "HAVCR2"),
    Regulatory   = c("FOXP3", "IL2RA", "CTLA4")
  ),
  quadrant_order  = c("NaiveLike", "Cytotoxic", "Regulatory", "Exhausted"),
  quadrant_labels = c("Naive",  "Cytotoxic",    # bl, br
                      "Treg",   "Exhausted"),    # tl, tr
  output_dir      = tempdir()
)

2.5 Split by donor or condition

split.by produces a separate figure per split level, each with its own axis limits. Use this to compare butterfly distributions across donors or conditions.

PlotPathwayButterfly(
  seurat_object  = bmcite,
  group.by       = "celltype.l1",
  split.by       = "donor",
  gene_sets      = list(
    Cytotoxic    = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG"),
    NaiveLike    = c("CCR7", "SELL", "TCF7"),
    Exhausted    = c("PDCD1", "LAG3", "HAVCR2"),
    Regulatory   = c("FOXP3", "IL2RA", "CTLA4")
  ),
  quadrant_order = c("NaiveLike", "Cytotoxic", "Regulatory", "Exhausted"),
  output_dir     = tempdir()
)

2.6 Handling large datasets

For objects with >50,000 cells, enable subsampling to keep scoring fast:

PlotPathwayButterfly(
  seurat_object     = large_obj,
  group.by          = "celltype",
  quadrant_order    = c("HALLMARK_MYC_TARGETS_V1",
                        "HALLMARK_E2F_TARGETS",
                        "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE",
                        "HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE"),
  subsample         = TRUE,
  n_cells           = 30000L,
  output_dir        = "./Figures/Butterfly"
)

Without subsampling, the function emits a warning at subsample_warn_at = 50000 cells (adjustable).

2.7 Return value

PlotPathwayButterfly invisibly returns a named list (one element per split level). Each element contains the raw coordinates and scores.

res <- PlotPathwayButterfly(
  seurat_object  = bmcite,
  group.by       = "celltype.l1",
  gene_sets      = list(
    Cytotoxic    = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG"),
    NaiveLike    = c("CCR7", "SELL", "TCF7"),
    Exhausted    = c("PDCD1", "LAG3", "HAVCR2"),
    Regulatory   = c("FOXP3", "IL2RA", "CTLA4")
  ),
  quadrant_order = c("NaiveLike", "Cytotoxic", "Regulatory", "Exhausted")
)

# Hierarchy coordinates (X, Y) for each cell
head(res$All$hierarchy)

# Raw pathway scores (cells x pathways)
head(res$All$scores)

PlotPathwayButterfly parameter reference

Parameter Type Default Description
seurat_object Seurat required Annotated Seurat object
group.by character required Metadata column for coloring
quadrant_order character(4) required Four pathway names: bottom-left, bottom-right, top-left, top-right
split.by character or NULL NULL One figure per level
gene_sets named list or NULL NULL Custom gene sets; NULL = fetch from MSigDB
pathway_sets named list Hallmark MSigDB collections when gene_sets = NULL
species character "Homo sapiens" Species for msigdbr
quadrant_labels character(4) or NULL NULL Corner display labels; NULL = auto-shortened pathway name
group_colors named character or NULL NULL Color map for group.by levels
assay character "RNA" Assay to extract counts from
layer character "counts" Layer/slot name
subsample logical FALSE Randomly sample cells before scoring
n_cells integer 20000 Cells to sample when subsample = TRUE
center logical TRUE Column-center scores before hierarchy transform
log_scale logical TRUE Apply log2 scaling to hierarchy coordinates
overview_size numeric 2 Width of overview panel relative to one small panel column
point_size numeric 0.3 Point size
point_alpha numeric 0.6 Point alpha
width numeric or NULL NULL PDF width (auto-sized when NULL)
height numeric or NULL NULL PDF height (auto-sized when NULL)
output_dir character or NULL NULL Save directory; NULL = print only
caffeinate logical FALSE Prevent system sleep

Part 3: RunEnrichment

3.1 Overview

RunEnrichment() runs over-representation analysis (ORA) via enrichR on a gene list or on significant DEGs from a data frame. It automatically calls PlotEnrichment() to save dot plots of enriched terms.

ORA is simpler than GSEA but answers a different question: given this gene list, which pathways contain more of these genes than expected by chance? No ranking is used - just presence or absence.

3.2 From a gene list

Pass a character vector of gene symbols directly.

RunEnrichment(
  features   = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG", "TNF", "FASLG", "NKG7"),
  databases  = c("MSigDB_Hallmark_2020",
                 "GO_Biological_Process_2023",
                 "KEGG_2021_Human"),
  output_dir = tempdir()
)

Run enrichR::listEnrichrDbs() for all available database names.

3.3 From a DEG table

Pass a DEG data frame; RunEnrichment filters for significant DEGs before testing. Columns are auto-detected from common RunWilcoxAUC/FindMarkers naming.

markers <- RunWilcoxAUC(bmcite, group.by = "celltype.l1")

RunEnrichment(
  deg_df     = markers,
  direction  = "up",         # "up", "down", or "both"
  fc_cutoff  = 0.25,
  padj_cutoff = 0.05,
  databases  = c("MSigDB_Hallmark_2020", "GO_Biological_Process_2023"),
  output_dir = tempdir(),
  object_name = "bmcite"
)

3.4 Per-group enrichment

When the DEG table has a group_column, RunEnrichment tests each group’s DEGs separately and saves one plot per group.

RunEnrichment(
  deg_df       = markers,
  group_column = "group",
  direction    = "up",
  databases    = c("MSigDB_Hallmark_2020"),
  top_n_plot   = 15L,
  output_dir   = tempdir(),
  object_name  = "bmcite"
)

3.5 Choosing databases

The default databases are MSigDB_Hallmark_2020, GO_Biological_Process_2023, and KEGG_2021_Human. You can test any databases available in enrichR.

# List all available enrichR databases
enrichR::listEnrichrDbs()

RunEnrichment(
  features  = c("GZMB", "PRF1", "IFNG"),
  databases = c("Reactome_2022",
                "WikiPathway_2023_Human",
                "CellMarker_Augmented_2021"),
  output_dir = tempdir()
)

3.6 Return value

RunEnrichment returns a nested list: [[group]][[database]] containing enrichR result data frames.

res <- RunEnrichment(
  deg_df       = markers,
  group_column = "group",
  direction    = "up",
  databases    = c("MSigDB_Hallmark_2020"),
  output_dir   = tempdir()
)

# Enrichment results for one group and database
head(res$`CD4 T`$MSigDB_Hallmark_2020)

RunEnrichment parameter reference

Parameter Type Default Description
features character or NULL NULL Gene list for ORA; use with OR deg_df
deg_df data frame or NULL NULL DEG table; genes filtered before testing
gene_column character or NULL NULL Gene column in deg_df; auto-detected if NULL
fc_column character or NULL NULL logFC column; auto-detected if NULL
padj_column character or NULL NULL Adjusted p-value column; auto-detected if NULL
group_column character or NULL NULL Group column in deg_df; NULL = test all together
fc_cutoff numeric 0.25 Minimum absolute logFC
padj_cutoff numeric 0.05 Maximum adjusted p-value
direction character "up" Which DEGs: "up", "down", or "both"
databases character vector Hallmark + GO:BP + KEGG enrichR databases to query
top_n_plot integer 20 Maximum terms per database to plot
plot_type character "dot" Plot type passed to PlotEnrichment
padj_cutoff_plot numeric 0.05 Significance cutoff for plot filtering
output_dir character or NULL NULL Save directory; NULL = print only
object_name character "" Prefix for output file names
subset_name character "" Optional subset label for file names

See also

  • Vignette 08: RunGSEA - rank-based GSEA on the full Seurat object
  • Vignette 09: RunGSEA_pseudobulk - sample-level pseudobulk GSEA with limma-voom
  • Vignette 05: GroupHeatmap / PlotDotPlot - gene-level expression visualizations
  • Vignette 11: RunCellChat - pathway-level cell-cell communication

Session info 

sessionInfo()
